# Extracao: transcription_m4a_Lesson_27_-_How_to_Stop_Making_Little_Exceptions_and_Hold_Yourself_Accountable

- **Fonte:** transcription_m4a_Lesson_27_-_How_to_Stop_Making_Little_Exceptions_and_Hold_Yourself_Accountable.txt
- **Tipo:** TXT
- **Tamanho:** 0 MB

---

In 2004, there were nine hospitals in the state of Michigan that implemented a simple procedure that made a dramatic improvement. And that procedure was that they set up a checklist in their intensive care unit that reminded physicians and nurses how to implement a central line. Just five steps, things like wash your hands, place a sterile sheet over the entire field. And these steps had been known for years. There was nothing new about them. There was nothing inventive about them. In fact, most people would say they were boring. Why are you reminding me to wash my hands? But by simply doing this one little action, by having this checklist in every little room in the ICU, they cut the infection rate by over 60 percent. Within 18 months, $75 million had been saved in expenses across the nine hospitals. And 1,500 lives were saved. And this idea that a checklist, something so simple and basic, could lead to such dramatic improvement. It kind of flies in the face of what we often think about medical innovations and what we need to save lives and improve the outcomes of our medical and hospital networks. But what's interesting about it is that the checklist is just an example of a feedback loop. It's just a way to measure or remind yourself what you are or aren't doing and then trigger the next action. And so by going down the checklist, you're measuring, did we wash our hands? Did we cover the sterile field? Did we make the adjustments that we need to make? So by doing that, they're reminding themselves in the moment and then informing the next action. Either, no, we didn't. So go ahead and wash your hands. Or yes, we did. So check it off and let's move to the next number. And this is a simple example of how you can build a feedback loop into your own life. Sometimes it can just be with the checklist. If you have things that you need to get done or if you have a certain procedure that you repeat over and over again, the same way that doctors and nurses put in these central lines over and over again, then a checklist can be really useful. But another one of my favorite ways to do this is with a strategy that I call the paperclip strategy. The paperclip strategy comes from a guy named Trent Dursman who at the time was a stockbroker 22, 23 years old. He was a young guy. Just made it out of school. And he was living in Abbotsford, Canada and working at a bank. Now this was in the mid 90s. So at the time, Abbotsford's this small little suburb of Vancouver. It certainly wasn't known as a financial capital by any means. And so Trent's boss came to him and just said, Trent, I want you to do the best job you can. But if you don't make a ton of sales, I'm not going to be expecting it from you. But here's your book of business. Here are your sales calls. Go do your job and see if you can make the company some money. Well, what's interesting is that over the next year, Trent outperformed all of his peers. And the way that he did it was through this simple paperclip strategy. He had one bin where in it he had 125 paperclips. And then he had another bin that was empty. And these two things sat on his desk each day. And he would make one sales call. And then he moved the paperclip to the other bin. And then he'd make another sales call and move the paperclip over. And he continued until all 125 paperclips removed each day. What's interesting about it is when I talked to Trent, he said, I didn't look at analyst reports. I didn't study the news or the weather. I didn't look at the trends in the stock market. I didn't try to predict what the market was going to do. I didn't even talk to my peers about what they thought good investments would be. I simply made my sales calls. And in any field of work, in any habit that we're trying to build, whether it's a sales habit or a health habit or a creativity habit, there's some core fundamental task, right? Some thing that is unsexy, just as unsexy as looking at a checklist in the hospital, that is essential to our progress, essential to our success. And if we can do that core fundamental thing, then we know that we're going to succeed. And that's all that Trent did. He built a system, a feedback loop, whether or not the paperclip had been moved over, to judge whether or not he was sticking to the fundamentals. And that's the action step that I would like you to take away from this video. How can you build some kind of visual cue, whether it's a checklist on a wall or a simple bin of paperclips being moved over, that measures your action, that gives you feedback on whether or not you're making progress? So often in life, we do things and we say, well, I think I wrote a lot of words today or I think I worked out hard today or I think I've worked out consistently over the course of a month. But we don't actually know because we haven't actually measured. We need some kind of measurement of our behavior if we're going to be self-aware and if we're going to inform the next action in an educated way.
